title: Denouncing the human trafficking and forced labor of and profiteering from Cuban medical personnel serving in third-world countries.
plain_summary: This bill says Cuba is doing something wrong with its doctors and nurses. Cuba sends these medical workers to help in other poor countries, but Cuba takes most of their money and controls where they can go. The US House of Representatives thinks this is like slavery and wants other countries to pay the Cuban medical workers directly instead of paying Cuba. The bill also asks the President to not let Cuban government officials visit the US if they help with this unfair treatment of medical workers.
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Foreign Affairs, and in addition to the Committee on the Judiciary, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provi

sections.title: Resolution Title
sections.text: This is a resolution that condemns the human trafficking and forced labor of Cuban medical workers who serve in poor countries around the world. It also condemns how Cuba makes money from these workers.

sections.title: Background Facts
sections.text: The State Department wrote a report in two thousand twenty four about human trafficking around the world. This report says Cuba is a Tier three country, which means Cuba does not meet basic standards to stop human trafficking and is not trying hard enough to fix this problem. The report says the Cuban government has a system where it sends workers to other countries and keeps most of their pay for itself. This includes sending doctors and other medical workers to foreign countries. The Cuban government takes away workers' passports and professional licenses. It watches workers closely and makes them follow strict rules about when they can leave their housing. The government does not always tell workers what their contracts say before they sign them. The report says Cuban officials help with serious human rights abuses and forced labor. The government threatens and punishes workers and their families if they try to leave these programs. Cuban authorities do not try to stop labor trafficking crimes. Another State Department report from June two thousand twenty four says Cuba makes between six billion and eight billion dollars every year from sending workers to other countries. This report lists seventy two countries that may have had Cuban government workers in the last five years. The report says other countries should use fair hiring practices when they hire foreign workers. These countries should also work to find victims of trafficking and protect them from more harm. However, Cuba still controls its workers who are sent to other countries. Foreign governments have refused to work with the United States to protect Cuban medical workers in their countries. A law from two thousand twenty four says the Secretary of State must use visa restrictions against officials from countries mentioned in the June report. The State Department has said that in Brazil, a Cuban medical program called Mais Medicos was helped by the Pan American Health Organization. Public information shows that Brazilian, Pan American Health Organization, and Cuban officials are responsible for creating and continuing the trafficking of Cuban doctors in the Mais Medicos program.

sections.title: House of Representatives Resolution
sections.text: The House of Representatives states the following five things. First, the human trafficking and forced labor of Cuban medical workers by the Cuban government are serious violations of international agreements about human trafficking and forced labor. Second, countries that signed these agreements have a serious duty to follow their promises and make sure Cuban medical workers have proper working conditions that meet international standards. Third, foreign governments should stop allowing Cuba to exploit workers and take their wages by paying Cuban medical workers directly instead of paying the Cuban government. Fourth, the President of the United States should use visa restrictions against foreign officials and organization officials who have helped Cuba's human trafficking plans. This includes Brazilian, Cuban, and Pan American Health Organization officials involved in Mais Medicos, and Honduran and Mexican officials involved in other similar plans. These restrictions should stay in place until the host country follows its duties under international human trafficking and forced labor agreements to protect workers' rights. Fifth, the House supports the policy changes described in a statement made on February twenty fifth, two thousand twenty five, by Secretary of State Marco Rubio. This statement was called Expansion of Visa Restrictions Policy for Individuals Exploiting Cuban Labor.
